raw_case:
The radiological perspective of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma: A case report
Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) is a rare, locally aggressive, highly vascularized benign tumor classically found in males from 13 to 20 years old. The most common clinical presentations are recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion. Imaging plays a significant role in differentiating and determining the extension of nasopharyngeal lesions. The case below highlights a 15-year-old male that presented with nasal congestion and recurrent epistaxis, who underwent nasal endoscopy and ablation. Persisting symptoms led to further evaluation by computed tomography and magnetic resonance imaging of the head; thus, the patient was diagnosed with JNA. Subsequently, the patient underwent angiographic embolization of the maxillary artery branches and eventually, endoscopic tumor resection. The presenting case is an example for physicians that might encounter an unsuspecting case of JNA to expedite diagnosis and care.

Introduction

Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma (JNA) is a benign, locally aggressive, highly vascularized tumor found in the posterior nasopharynx and sphenopalatine foramen. Males ages 13 to 20 are the most affected with an incidence of up to 1 in 50,000 [1]. Though locally invasive to surrounding bony structures, malignant changes are rare. The etiological origin of the tumor does not have a clear consensus, but studies have suggested origins from a hamartomatous nidus in the roof of the nasopharynx, adjacent to the sphenopalatine foramen [2,3]. The cellular makeup of the lesion is mostly fibrous, with a large vascular component that lacks a muscular layer, thus eliminating the ability of the vessels to contract in response to a physiological injury, hence causing severe epistaxis [4]. Immunohistochemical staining can be done for a variety of vascular markers such as VEGF, CD34, and PDGF. Immunohistochemistry has shown that JNA cells highly express androgen receptors, making them testosterone dependent and thus proliferate during puberty in adolescent males [5]. Vascular supply of the lesion most commonly originates from the external carotid artery via internal maxillary or ascending pharyngeal arteries. Larger lesions can have supply by the internal carotid artery via the sphenoidal or ophthalmic artery [6]. Patients can present with unilateral nasal obstruction or epistaxis in 91% and 63% of cases, respectively [7].Although nasal endoscopy is frequently performed for patients with recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion, differentiating JNA from other etiologies is not always possible through direct visualization. Radiological investigation demonstrates the highly vascularized, locally aggressive nature of this lesion, as well as its extension, and eliminates the need for biopsy sampling. Further evaluation with cerebral angiography and presurgical devascularization also plays a significant role in JNA treatment. There are reports highlighting imaging characteristics of JNA in systematic reviews, demonstrating the variability in CT/MRI presentation [8]. Notable conclusions were involvement of the sphenopalatine foramen and pterygopalatine fossa in 76% of cases, high signal intensity in 61.1%, and mean DWI of 2.07 × 10-3 mm2/s. All measurements mentioned from this resource are consistent with the reported patient in this case. Research on JNA highlights systematic reviews to aid in understanding common presenting symptoms, which is important to consider regarding epidemiology and warning symptoms requiring further investigation [1]. Other recent research shows histopathological findings noted within the lesion, such as fibrous stroma alongside extensive blood vessel involvement [3].The case presented highlights the necessity of imaging modalities to further characterize nasopharyngeal lesions in patients with nonspecific symptoms as epistaxis and nasal congestion. Early diagnosis of JNA can help prevent massive blood loss, improve quality of life, and expedite care time.

Case report

Encounters began when a 15-year-old male presented with a 4-year history of recurrent mild epistaxis. The patient did not endorse any history of smoking or tobacco use, blood thinner usage, anemia, underlying heart conditions, or use of antihypertensive medications. The patient had a history of seasonal allergy, managed by second generation antihistamine. He reported daily epistaxis episodes, involving the bilateral nasal passages being worse on the right, lasting for 1 hour on average for the past 4 weeks. Physical exam revealed a midline nasal septum. The prominent intranasal vessels on the right side were treated with silver nitrate cautery which improved the patient's symptoms temporarily.About 13 months later, the patient reported multiple epistaxis episodes each lasting 1 hour, for 2 days, and with episodes being worse on the left side. Physical exam on this visit marked a left deviation of the septum that was not noted the year prior. The patient had left nasal cavity prominent vessels cauterized with silver nitrate and was advised to follow up as needed.After 4 months, the patient returned with minimal exacerbation of symptoms yielding a septal deviation to the left with spurring anteriorly, mucosal edema, congestion and rhinorrhea, with clots presented in the bilateral nasal cavities. The family reported a history of snoring, but no gross nasal deformity was ascertained. At this point, the care team decided to perform a nasal endoscopy which identified left septal spurring, inferior turbinate hypertrophy, polypoid swelling in the middle meatus and nasal airway, purulence inferior nasal floor on right, and a soft tissue swelling obstructing right nasopharynx. Findings were suggestive of adenoid hypertrophy and/or adenoiditis and the patient was treated with a course of doxycycline, prednisone, and saline spray.The following visit, the patient reported epistaxis episodes, intranasal clots, and a noticeable right nasal obstruction. The nasal obstruction was impacting the patient's breathing and daily activities. A noncontrast CT of the sinuses was obtained (Fig. 1), which revealed a right sided soft tissue density mass causing widening of the right pterygopalatine fossa. Globulation of the lesions margins and bowing of the posterior wall of the right maxillary sinus was noted. Despite the expansile feature of the lesion, no aggressive osseous destruction was demonstrated. Differential diagnosis including juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, nasopharyngeal polyp, and rhabdomyosarcoma were discussed and further evaluation with contrast enhanced MRI was recommended.Contrast enhanced MRI of the head (Fig. 2) revealed a 4.3 × 2.8 × 4.9 cm avidly enhancing mass arising within and expanding the right sphenopalatine foramen with extension into the posterior nasal cavity and posterior nasopharynx. The lesion demonstrated intermediate signal intensity on T2 images and presented numerous internal flow voids that confirmed a highly vascularized mass. The patient's diffusion weighted imaging (DWI) (Fig. 3) revealed a high Apparent Diffusion Coefficient (ADC) value of 2.177 × 10-3 mm2/s, indicating increased diffusion of water molecules. The combination of primary site, multiple internal flow voids indicating internal vascular sources, and the high ADC on imaging ruled out malignant causes in the differential and confirmed the diagnosis of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma.After the diagnosis was made, the patient was consulted about surgery and elected to proceed. The surgery consisted of a transoral approach requiring preoperative embolization of the right internal maxillary artery and planned sacrifice of the right vidian artery and nerve. The patient underwent cerebral digital subtraction angiography 24 hours prior to the JNA debulking surgery, and particle embolization of the nasal and posterior nasopharyngeal branches of the right maxillary artery was performed (Fig. 4).The patient then underwent tumor resection by an endoscopic modified craniofacial approach. The vascular tumor was noted to originate posterior to the right middle turbinate. This was tethered at the palatine bone likely from the right maxillary artery within the pterygopalatine fossa, with attachments extending along the face of the sphenoid, the choana, and the posterior septum. There was a portion of the tumor that tracked along the right vidian nerve and artery into the sphenoid sinus that was resected separately once the main tumor had been delivered. The tumor was then retrieved using a sweetheart retractor and passed off for permanent section (Fig. 5).At the post operative appointment, the patient endorsed improvement in his symptoms including difficulty breathing and snoring. He has not had any epistaxis episodes since surgery. A postoperative nasal endoscopy showed appropriately healing mucosa along the posterior wall of the right maxillary sinus extending towards the pterygopalatine fossa contents. A postoperative contrast enhanced MRI of the head (Fig. 6) demonstrates interval resection of the previously identified juvenile nasal angiofibroma with no evidence of residual or recurrent tumor. Mild chronic inflammatory changes are noted within the paranasal sinuses.

Discussion

In summary, a 15-year-old male presented with a 4-year history of epistaxis which were temporarily controlled with cautery. Endoscopic evaluation yielded an initial diagnosis of nasal polyposis, but imaging modalities including CT and MRI, followed by a diagnostic/therapeutic cerebral angiogram concluded the most likely diagnosis of JNA. The significance of the case highlights the long list of differentials for a case of JNA, and how appropriate imaging can allow for a proper diagnosis.The noncontrast sinus CT revealed a lobulated soft tissue mass arising within the right sphenopalatine foramen with associated osseous expansion and remodeling. This is a well-documented feature of JNA, along with the bowing of the posterior wall of the maxillary sinus [9]. MR evaluation of the lesion demonstrated T1 isointensity, and the T2 TSE images demonstrated hyper to iso signal intensity mass in comparison to the musculature signal intensity, with numerous internal flow voids, suggesting a high degree of vascularity. T1 postgadolinium images revealed briskly enhancing soft tissue mass arising within and expanding the right sphenopalatine foramen. These MRI findings are consistent with previous studies [10,11]. Baba et al. described T1 partial high signal intensity in 61.1% of their JNA cases with cystic changes in 40.8% of the JNA lesions. JNA lesions were described to show T2 hyper to isointensity in comparison to the musculature signal intensity.To best differentiate JNA from other soft tissue masses in the sinonasal region, whether benign or malignant, the gold standard pathognomic factors to consider are ADC values and number of internal flow voids. As this patient's ADC value of 2.177 × 10-3 mm2/s, it was consistent with the reported average ADC value of 2.169 ± 0.270 × 10-3 mm2/s for JNA lesions by the Das et al study [12]. To evaluate this deeper, a higher ADC indicates an increased movement of water molecules through tissue. Discovering this characteristic on DWI provided a key clue revealing that this tumor was actually not densely packed with cells, but primarily consisted of loose fibrous stroma and rich vascular network. This would account for increased diffusion of these water molecules and thus a higher ADC value [12]. In addition to ADC, on MR imaging there can be rapidly flowing blood within large vessels that do not elicit a signal, thus appearing dark and constituting internal flow voids. Given JNA's cell of origin being vascular tissue, these tumors possess significant blood supply from the external carotid artery and its branches. Catching multiple internal flow voids on MRI is the hallmark of JNA diagnostic criteria and for this patient, effectively excluded other possible etiologies that do not have the same pronounced vascular architecture of JNA.The DSA showed contributions of the right maxillary artery to the lesion, which is reported as the most common artery involved [13]. Cerebral angiography and particle embolization was performed 24 hours prior to the debulking surgery which is within the recommended time interval of 24-72 hours window [14].Other differential diagnoses of recurrent epistaxis and nasal obstruction include nasal polyposis, rhabdomyosarcoma, and nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Nasal polyposis on endoscopy presents usually with bilateral inflammatory polyps, and unilateral presentation requires additional imaging due to risk of malignancy. On MRI, nasal polyposis can present as a nasopharyngeal mass with heterogeneous T2 signal intensity that does not infiltrate surrounding tissues and rarely have significant internal flow voids [15]. Rhabdomyosarcoma in the head and neck more often presents with facial pain and congestion, with possible ocular compression and aggressive growth into surrounding sinuses [16]. On MRI, these rhabdomyosarcomas possess T1 isointensity and T2 hyperintensity with low ADCs of 0.72 × 10-3 mm2/s, indicating a higher cellularity content restricting water diffusion within the tissue [6]. The tumor rarely has identifiable borders, and is associated with local lymph node enlargement [16]. Additionally, rhabdomyosarcomas rarely has any pronounced internal flow voids as it is typically a solid mass with low vascularization. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma presents mostly along the nasopharyngeal wall with infiltration into surrounding tissues. On MRI, nasopharyngeal carcinoma most commonly presents as a homogenous mass with hypo T1 and T2 signal intensity [15]. Studies have determined nasopharyngeal carcinoma tumors to also have a low ADC value on DWI, ranging from 0.713 × 10-3 to 0.716 × 10-3 mm2/s due to their increased cellularity [17]. Additionally, these tumors also usually lack prominent internal flow voids given their solid cellular nature and propensity for cellular necrosis. In more advanced stages of nasopharyngeal carcinoma, its vascular supply is classically unorganized and still not to the same extent of flow voids seen in JNAs.In patients with JNA a thorough clinical history is needed for suspicion, and proper imaging is required for diagnosis. The radiology focused aspect of this case highlights classic findings for JNA in a patient that was misdiagnosed for multiple months. Current literature lacks a universal radiographic characterization of classically occurring JNA, nor established criteria for excluding other differential diagnoses. Going forward, it is the hope that this case report will bring attention to the need for a recognized protocol on JNA evaluation for the field of radiology.

Conclusion

This case highlights how imaging can be critical in diagnosis of the patients with recurrent nonspecific symptoms such as epistaxis and nasal obstruction and their treatment trajectory. While this case serves as an example of classic JNA presentation and imaging findings to be referenced by clinicians, it also provides an in-depth analysis into the workup of such nasopharyngeal mass for radiologists. The practical approach for radiologists faced with similar cases is to begin with evaluating anatomical origin of the tumor on CT imaging, followed by thoroughly assessing the tumor's ADC value on DWI and number of internal flow voids, and then correlating findings clinically to establish the most accurate diagnosis of JNA. Future research implications could involve JNA specific endoscopic findings to better identify the tumor over other differential diagnoses such as polyposis via direct visualization.

Patient consent

This letter is to certify that written informed consent has been obtained for this case report.Consent was signed by the living patient and his mother for publication of this case. The consent form includes use of their story, CT scans, MRI scans, and gross tumor. In return, the patient will remain anonymous.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 15-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion
- **History of Present Illness:** 4-year history of recurrent mild epistaxis, worsening over time with daily episodes lasting 1 hour in the past month. Temporary symptom relief with silver nitrate cautery but recurring episodes, nasal congestion, and clots persisted.
- **Past Medical History:** Seasonal allergy managed with antihistamines.
- **Personal History:** No smoking, tobacco use, blood thinner use, hypertension, or anemia.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Prominent intranasal vessels on the right side (initially), later progressing to left deviation of the nasal septum, mucosal edema, nasal congestion, and obstructions. Polypoid swelling and soft tissue mass obstructing the right nasopharynx on nasal endoscopy.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Noncontrast CT scan: Right-sided soft tissue mass causing widening of the pterygopalatine fossa, no osseous destruction.
  - Contrast-enhanced MRI: Avidly enhancing 4.3 × 2.8 × 4.9 cm mass in the right sphenopalatine foramen, numerous internal flow voids, high ADC value of 2.177 × 10-3 mm2/s.
  - Digital subtraction angiography: Vascular supply from the right maxillary artery.
  - Pathology from resected tumor: Consistent with juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Clinical Presentation:** Recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion prompted an evaluation for common causes such as nasal polyposis, adenoiditis, or septal abnormalities. Temporary symptom improvement with cautery and antibiotics initially left suspicion for benign inflammatory causes.
   
2. **Imaging Evaluation:**
   - CT findings of a lobulated soft tissue mass in the right sphenopalatine foramen prompted a differential diagnosis of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA), nasopharyngeal polyp, and rhabdomyosarcoma.
   - MRI confirmed the vascular nature of the mass, as evidenced by internal flow voids and the high ADC value, which supports the diagnosis of JNA and excludes malignancies like rhabdomyosarcoma and nasopharyngeal carcinoma, which typically have low ADC values due to high cellularity.

3. **Exclusion of Other Diagnoses:**
   - **Nasal polyposis:** Unlikely due to the unilateral presentation and vascularized appearance on MRI.
   - **Rhabdomyosarcoma:** Excluded by MRI findings of numerous flow voids and a high ADC, whereas rhabdomyosarcoma typically shows low ADC due to cellular density.
   - **Nasopharyngeal carcinoma:** Rule-out supported by lack of lymphadenopathy, homogeneous T1 intensity, low ADC, and necrosis, which are characteristic of carcinoma.

4. **Final Confirmation:** Diagnosis of JNA was confirmed by imaging findings correlating with typical characteristics (vascular structure, sphenopalatine foramen origin, high ADC).

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The final diagnosis is **juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA)** based on clinical findings, imaging characteristics (highly vascular mass with internal flow voids and high ADC on MRI), and pathology from tumor resection.

---

results.content:
### Reasoning:
In approaching this case, I need to consider the patient's symptoms, medical history, physical examination findings, and the results of ancillary tests to arrive at a diagnosis.

First, the patient is a 15-year-old male presenting with a 4-year history of recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion. The fact that he's male and in this age group is important because certain conditions are more prevalent in adolescence, particularly in males.

The epistaxis has been recurrent and mild for four years but has worsened recently, with daily episodes lasting an hour. This suggests that there may be an underlying pathological condition causing the bleeding, rather than just occasional trauma or dryness.

Silver nitrate cautery provided temporary relief, which indicates that the bleeding might be originating from a specific site that can be cauterized, but since the episodes recur, it's likely that the underlying cause hasn't been addressed.

The patient has a history of seasonal allergies managed with antihistamines, which could contribute to nasal congestion but is less likely to cause recurrent epistaxis.

In the physical examination, prominent intranasal vessels were noted on the right side initially, which progressed to left deviation of the nasal septum, mucosal edema, nasal congestion, and obstructions. A polypoid swelling and soft tissue mass were observed obstructing the right nasopharynx on nasal endoscopy.

These findings suggest that there may be a mass lesion in the nasopharynx causing the symptoms. The deviation of the nasal septum could be related to the mass effect of the tumor.

The ancillary tests provide more specific information. The noncontrast CT scan showed a right-sided soft tissue mass causing widening of the pterygopalatine fossa without osseous destruction. This indicates that there is a significant mass effect but no evidence of bone invasion or destruction.

The contrast-enhanced MRI revealed an avidly enhancing mass measuring 4.3 x 2.8 x 4.9 cm in the right sphenopalatine foramen, with numerous internal flow voids and a high ADC value. The high ADC value suggests that the lesion has high water content, which is consistent with vascular lesions.

Digital subtraction angiography showed that the vascular supply to the mass is from the right maxillary artery, which is a branch of the external carotid artery. This is important for planning any surgical intervention, as it helps identify the blood supply to the tumor.

Finally, the pathology from the resected tumor is consistent with juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma.

Given all this information, I need to consider the differential diagnoses for a young male with recurrent epistaxis, nasal congestion, and a nasopharyngeal mass.

Potential differential diagnoses include:

1. Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA): This is a benign but locally aggressive vascular tumor that occurs almost exclusively in adolescent males. It can cause epistaxis, nasal obstruction, and mass effects in the nasopharynx. The age and sex of the patient, along with the location and vascular characteristics of the mass, make JNA a likely diagnosis.

2. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma: Although rare in adolescents, it's still a consideration, especially in regions where Epstein-Barr virus is endemic. However, nasopharyngeal carcinoma is more common in adults, particularly in Southeast Asia.

3. Inverted papilloma: This is a benign tumor that can occur in the nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses. It can cause nasal obstruction and sometimes bleeding but is less aggressive than JNA.

4. Hemangioma: A vascular tumor that can cause epistaxis and nasal congestion. However, hemangiomas are less common in this location and typically present differently.

5. Lymphoma: Primary nasopharyngeal lymphoma is rare, but it can present with similar symptoms.

Given the patient's age, sex, symptoms, and the pathology report confirming juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, it seems that JNA is the most likely diagnosis.

To confirm this, I would consider the following:

- Review the pathology report in detail to ensure that it is indeed consistent with JNA. Look for the characteristic histological features such as fibrous stroma with vascular channels and hyperplastic endothelium.

- Consider the radiological findings, which show a vascular mass with enhancement and flow voids, consistent with the high vascularity of JNA.

- Consider the patient's age and sex, as JNA typically affects adolescent males.

- Evaluate the treatment history, including the response to cauterization, which provided only temporary relief, suggesting that the underlying tumor was still present.

In conclusion, based on the clinical presentation, radiological findings, and pathology report, the diagnosis is juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma.

### Answer:
Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma

Digital subtraction angiography showed that the vascular supply to the mass is from the right maxillary artery, which is a branch of the external carotid artery. This is important for planning any surgical intervention, as it helps identify the blood supply to the tumor.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> The patient presented with recurrent epistaxis and nasal congestion, initially evaluated for common causes such as nasal polyposis, adenoiditis, or septal abnormalities, with temporary symptom improvement following cautery and antibiotics suggesting benign inflammatory causes.

<Step 2> CT imaging revealed a lobulated soft tissue mass in the right sphenopalatine foramen, prompting a differential diagnosis of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA), nasopharyngeal polyp, and rhabdomyosarcoma.

<Step 3> MRI confirmed the vascular nature of the mass with internal flow voids and a high ADC value, supporting the diagnosis of JNA and excluding malignancies like rhabdomyosarcoma and nasopharyngeal carcinoma, which typically have low ADC values due to high cellularity.
<Step 4> Nasal polyposis was excluded due to the unilateral presentation and the vascularized appearance of the mass on MRI.
<Step 5> Rhabdomyosarcoma was excluded based on MRI findings of numerous flow voids and a high ADC, as rhabdomyosarcoma typically shows low ADC due to high cellular density.
<Step 6> Nasopharyngeal carcinoma was ruled out due to the absence of lymphadenopathy, homogeneous T1 intensity, high ADC, and lack of necrosis, which are characteristic of carcinoma.
<Step 7> The diagnosis of JNA was confirmed by imaging findings consistent with its typical characteristics, including a vascular structure, origin in the sphenopalatine foramen, and high ADC.
<Step 8> The final diagnosis is juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA), based on clinical findings, imaging characteristics, and pathology from tumor resection.
